question: What is the safe minimum internal temperature for chicken and poultry?
What is the safe minimum internal temperature for whole cuts of pork?
What is the safe minimum internal temperature for ground beef and minced meat?
What is the safe minimum internal temperature for whole beef steaks and roasts?
answers: 63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest
74°C (165°F) with a 5-minute rest
57°C (135°F) with a 1-minute rest
71°C (160°F) with no rest required
correct: 63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest

question: At what internal temperature is a beef steak considered medium-rare?
What internal temperature indicates a beef steak cooked to medium?
What is the safe minimum internal temperature for fish and seafood?
What internal temperature should a whole roast turkey reach?
answers: 74°C (165°F) in the thickest part of the thigh
63°C (145°F) measured at the breast centre
82°C (180°F) measured at the tip of the drumstick
68°C (155°F) in the stuffing cavity if present
correct: 74°C (165°F) in the thickest part of the thigh

question: What is the safe minimum internal temperature for lamb chops and whole cuts?
answers: 63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest
74°C (165°F) with a 5-minute rest
57°C (135°F) with a 1-minute rest
71°C (160°F) with no rest required
correct: 63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest

question: At what temperature is pork considered well done?
answers: 77°C (170°F) or above throughout
63°C (145°F) with a short rest period
71°C (160°F) with no rest needed
85°C (185°F) throughout the whole cut
correct: 77°C (170°F) or above throughout

question: What internal temperature should a pre-cooked ham reach when reheated?
What temperature indicates duck breast cooked to a safe medium doneness?
answers: 74°C (165°F) at the thickest point
63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest
57°C (135°F) measured at the centre
82°C (180°F) throughout the whole breast
correct: 74°C (165°F) at the thickest point

question: At what internal temperature are egg-based dishes considered safe to eat?
What is the danger zone temperature range in which bacteria multiply rapidly in food?
What internal temperature should raw sausages reach to be safe to eat?
answers: 71°C (160°F) throughout
63°C (145°F) throughout
74°C (165°F) throughout
68°C (155°F) throughout
correct: 71°C (160°F) throughout

question: What is the correct internal temperature for a medium-well beef steak?
At what temperature should cooked food be held hot to stay safe for service?
answers: Above 60°C (140°F)
Above 40°C (104°F)
Above 50°C (122°F)
Above 74°C (165°F)
correct: Above 60°C (140°F)

question: What is the recommended internal temperature for veal cutlets and roasts?
answers: 63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest
74°C (165°F) with a 5-minute rest
71°C (160°F) with no rest required
57°C (135°F) with a 1-minute rest
correct: 63°C (145°F) with a 3-minute rest

question: At what internal temperature is beef considered well done?
answers: 77°C (170°F) and above
63°C (145°F) and above
74°C (165°F) and above
68°C (155°F) and above
correct: 77°C (170°F) and above

question: What is the correct internal temperature for a rare beef steak?
What are the two ingredients in a classic roux?
answers: Equal parts plain flour and butter by weight, cooked together
Equal parts plain flour and cream by weight, whisked cold
Equal parts cornflour and butter by weight, simmered briefly
Equal parts plain flour and oil by volume, cooked in a pan
correct: Equal parts plain flour and butter by weight, cooked together

question: What are the three types of roux distinguished by colour?
answers: White, blond (pale), and brown — determined by how long the roux is cooked
Light, medium, and dark — determined by the ratio of flour to butter used
Thin, standard, and thick — determined by the amount of liquid added
Cold, warm, and hot — determined by the temperature of the butter used
correct: White, blond (pale), and brown — determined by how long the roux is cooked

question: What are the ingredients in a classic béchamel sauce?
answers: White roux, warm whole milk, salt, pepper, and a pinch of nutmeg
Brown roux, beef stock, tomato paste, and a bouquet garni
Blond roux, white wine, double cream, and fresh tarragon
White roux, warm chicken stock, shallots, and dry white wine
correct: White roux, warm whole milk, salt, pepper, and a pinch of nutmeg

question: What is a velouté sauce made from?
answers: A blond roux whisked into a light white stock (chicken, veal, or fish)
A white roux whisked into warm whole milk with nutmeg and seasoning
A brown roux simmered with brown stock and a classic mirepoix base
Egg yolks emulsified with clarified butter and seasoned with lemon
correct: A blond roux whisked into a light white stock (chicken, veal, or fish)

question: What are the five French mother sauces?
answers: Béchamel, velouté, espagnole, sauce tomat, and hollandaise
Béchamel, hollandaise, mayonnaise, velouté, and vinaigrette
Espagnole, béchamel, chasseur, bordelaise, and béarnaise
Hollandaise, béchamel, demi-glace, roux, and velouté
correct: Béchamel, velouté, espagnole, sauce tomat, and hollandaise

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic hollandaise sauce?
answers: Egg yolks, clarified butter, lemon juice, and seasoning
Whole eggs, double cream, white wine, and tarragon
Egg yolks, olive oil, white wine vinegar, and mustard
Egg whites, unsalted butter, lime juice, and cayenne
correct: Egg yolks, clarified butter, lemon juice, and seasoning

question: What is the difference between hollandaise and béarnaise sauce?
answers: Béarnaise replaces lemon juice with a tarragon and vinegar reduction
Béarnaise uses whole eggs instead of yolks and adds fresh chervil
Béarnaise is made with olive oil rather than clarified butter
Béarnaise adds tomato concassé and uses red wine instead of lemon
correct: Béarnaise replaces lemon juice with a tarragon and vinegar reduction

question: What are the ingredients in a classic mayonnaise?
answers: Egg yolks, neutral oil, mustard, lemon juice or vinegar, and seasoning
Whole eggs, olive oil, tarragon vinegar, capers, and white pepper
Egg yolks, double cream, white wine, Dijon mustard, and herbs
Egg whites, vegetable oil, apple cider vinegar, and garlic paste
correct: Egg yolks, neutral oil, mustard, lemon juice or vinegar, and seasoning

question: What is espagnole (Spanish sauce) made from?
answers: Brown roux, brown veal stock, mirepoix, tomato paste, and a bouquet garni
Blond roux, chicken stock, white wine, shallots, and fresh parsley
White roux, warm milk, onion studded with cloves, and bay leaf
Egg yolks emulsified with brown butter, sherry, and smoked paprika
correct: Brown roux, brown veal stock, mirepoix, tomato paste, and a bouquet garni

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic vinaigrette?
answers: Three parts oil to one part acid (vinegar or citrus), mustard, and seasoning
Equal parts oil and vinegar with honey, garlic, and fresh thyme
Two parts olive oil to one part lemon, with capers and anchovies
Four parts oil to one part acid with cream, shallots, and chives
correct: Three parts oil to one part acid (vinegar or citrus), mustard, and seasoning

question: What is a mirepoix?
answers: A rough-cut mixture of onion, carrot, and celery used to build flavour in stocks and sauces
A finely diced mixture of shallots, garlic, and leeks used as a sauce base
A julienned mixture of vegetables used specifically as a garnish for consommé
A roasted mixture of root vegetables blended into a thick vegetable purée
correct: A rough-cut mixture of onion, carrot, and celery used to build flavour in stocks and sauces

question: What is a bouquet garni?
answers: A bundle of fresh herbs (typically bay, thyme, and parsley stalks) tied together or wrapped in muslin
A blend of dried ground spices tied in muslin and simmered in stocks and braises
A garnish of fresh herbs placed on a dish immediately before it is served at table
A mixture of aromatic vegetables wrapped in leek leaves and tied with kitchen string
correct: A bundle of fresh herbs (typically bay, thyme, and parsley stalks) tied together or wrapped in muslin

question: What are the ingredients in a classic beef stock (brown stock)?
answers: Roasted beef bones, mirepoix, tomato paste, bouquet garni, and cold water
Raw chicken carcasses, white mirepoix, leeks, celery, and cold water
Veal bones, roasted onions, red wine, thyme, and hot water
Beef mince, aromatics, tomatoes, red wine vinegar, and cold water
correct: Roasted beef bones, mirepoix, tomato paste, bouquet garni, and cold water

question: What ingredients make up a classic chicken stock?
answers: Raw chicken carcasses, onion, carrot, celery, leek, peppercorns, and cold water
Roasted chicken bones, mirepoix, red wine, tomato paste, and hot water
Whole chicken, root vegetables, bay leaf, thyme, and boiling water
Chicken wings, brown butter, shallots, garlic, and white wine stock
correct: Raw chicken carcasses, onion, carrot, celery, leek, peppercorns, and cold water

question: What is the ratio of fat to flour in a standard roux?
answers: Equal parts by weight (1:1)
Two parts fat to one part flour by weight
One part fat to two parts flour by weight
Three parts fat to one part flour by weight
correct: Equal parts by weight (1:1)

question: What are the three main ingredients in a classic Caesar dressing?
answers: Egg yolks (or mayo), anchovies, Parmesan, lemon, garlic, and Worcestershire
Double cream, Parmesan, lemon juice, capers, and black pepper
Olive oil, red wine vinegar, Parmesan, garlic, and Dijon mustard
Egg yolks, blue cheese, buttermilk, chives, and white wine vinegar
correct: Egg yolks (or mayo), anchovies, Parmesan, lemon, garlic, and Worcestershire

question: What is a liaison in cooking?
answers: A mixture of egg yolks and cream used to enrich and thicken sauces and soups
A sauce made from equal parts butter and flour kneaded together cold
A starch-based thickener made from cornflour dissolved in cold liquid
A reduction of wine and shallots used as a base for butter sauces
correct: A mixture of egg yolks and cream used to enrich and thicken sauces and soups

question: What is beurre blanc?
answers: A French butter sauce made by emulsifying cold butter into a white wine and shallot reduction
Clarified butter infused with lemon juice and fresh herbs for finishing fish dishes
Browned butter (beurre noisette) whisked with cream and seasoned with lemon
Softened butter blended with herbs, garlic, and lemon to form a compound butter
correct: A French butter sauce made by emulsifying cold butter into a white wine and shallot reduction

question: What is a demi-glace?
answers: A rich sauce made by reducing espagnole and brown stock together by half until glossy
A thick sauce made from a dark roux cooked until it reaches a chocolate colour
A concentrated glaze made by reducing chicken stock until it coats the back of a spoon
A reduction of red wine, shallots, and bone marrow used only with grilled beef
correct: A rich sauce made by reducing espagnole and brown stock together by half until glossy

question: What is the difference between sautéing and frying?
answers: Sautéing uses a small amount of fat over high heat with frequent movement; frying uses a larger quantity of fat with the food left relatively still
Sautéing is always done in butter while frying uses only oil; the temperatures used are essentially the same for both methods
Sautéing is a slow, low-heat method for tenderising tough cuts; frying is always done at very high heat for quick caramelisation
Sautéing requires a lid to trap steam; frying is always uncovered to allow moisture to escape from the food
correct: Sautéing uses a small amount of fat over high heat with frequent movement; frying uses a larger quantity of fat with the food left relatively still

question: What is the Maillard reaction?
answers: A chemical reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars that creates brown colour and complex flavour when food is heated
The process by which proteins in meat denature and contract when exposed to high cooking temperatures
A reaction between fat and oxygen that produces the characteristic smell of rancid or over-cooked food
The caramelisation of natural sugars in vegetables when they are roasted at temperatures above 180°C
correct: A chemical reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars that creates brown colour and complex flavour when food is heated

question: What does it mean to 'blanch' a vegetable?
answers: To briefly cook it in boiling salted water then immediately plunge it into ice water to stop cooking and preserve colour
To cook it slowly in butter over low heat until tender but without any colour developing on the surface
To roast it dry in a hot oven until the outer skin is charred and the interior is soft
To steam it in a covered pan with a small amount of water and butter until just tender
correct: To briefly cook it in boiling salted water then immediately plunge it into ice water to stop cooking and preserve colour

question: What is braising?
answers: A method of cooking tough cuts in a small amount of liquid in a covered vessel at low heat for a long time
A high-heat method of cooking in a very small amount of fat, turning the food frequently in the pan
A technique of cooking food submerged in hot fat at a controlled temperature in a deep vessel
A method of cooking food slowly in a water bath held at a precise temperature below boiling
correct: A method of cooking tough cuts in a small amount of liquid in a covered vessel at low heat for a long time

question: What is the difference between poaching, simmering, and boiling?
answers: Poaching 65–80°C (gentle movement); simmering 85–95°C (small bubbles); boiling 100°C (vigorous rolling)
Poaching 50–60°C (no movement); simmering 70–80°C (rare bubbles); boiling 90°C (steady bubbles)
Poaching 75–85°C (medium bubbles); simmering 95–98°C (rolling); boiling 100°C (rapid rolling)
Poaching 55–65°C (no movement); simmering 80–90°C (fast rolling); boiling 100°C (vigorous steam)
correct: Poaching 65–80°C (gentle movement); simmering 85–95°C (small bubbles); boiling 100°C (vigorous rolling)

question: What is clarified butter (ghee)?
answers: Butter that has been melted and had the milk solids and water removed, leaving pure butterfat
Butter that has been whipped with air until light and spreadable at room temperature
Butter cooked until the milk solids brown, giving a nutty aroma and golden colour
Butter blended with neutral oil to raise its smoke point for use in high-heat cooking
correct: Butter that has been melted and had the milk solids and water removed, leaving pure butterfat

question: What is the difference between beurre noisette and beurre noir?
answers: Beurre noisette is butter cooked to a light golden-brown; beurre noir is cooked until dark brown, both finished with acid
Beurre noisette uses unsalted butter clarified slowly; beurre noir uses salted butter cooked over very high heat
Beurre noisette is a cold butter sauce; beurre noir is a warm brown butter emulsified with cream
Beurre noisette is browned butter with herbs; beurre noir is browned butter thickened with a roux
correct: Beurre noisette is butter cooked to a light golden-brown; beurre noir is cooked until dark brown, both finished with acid

question: What is julienne as a knife cut?
answers: Long thin matchstick strips approximately 3mm × 3mm × 6cm
Small uniform cubes approximately 6mm × 6mm × 6mm
Very fine dice approximately 1.5mm × 1.5mm × 1.5mm
Thin diagonal slices approximately 3mm thick cut on the bias
correct: Long thin matchstick strips approximately 3mm × 3mm × 6cm

question: What is a brunoise knife cut?
answers: Very fine dice approximately 1.5mm × 1.5mm × 1.5mm, created from julienne strips
Small standard dice approximately 6mm × 6mm × 6mm for general use
Long thin matchstick strips approximately 3mm × 3mm × 6cm in length
Medium dice approximately 12mm × 12mm × 12mm for soups and stews
correct: Very fine dice approximately 1.5mm × 1.5mm × 1.5mm, created from julienne strips

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic beef Wellington?
answers: Beef fillet, mushroom duxelles, Parma ham or prosciutto, and puff pastry
Beef sirloin, caramelised onions, Gruyère cheese, and shortcrust pastry
Beef topside, spinach, blue cheese, mushrooms, and filo pastry
Beef fillet, red wine reduction, liver pâté, and rough puff pastry
correct: Beef fillet, mushroom duxelles, Parma ham or prosciutto, and puff pastry

question: What is duxelles?
answers: Finely chopped mushrooms cooked with shallots and butter until all moisture has evaporated
A mixture of finely diced carrots, onions, and celery cooked in butter as an aromatic base
Sliced mushrooms sautéed briefly in butter and seasoned with garlic and fresh thyme
A mushroom purée blended with cream and truffle oil used to fill pastry shells
correct: Finely chopped mushrooms cooked with shallots and butter until all moisture has evaporated

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic eggs Benedict?
answers: Toasted English muffin, Canadian bacon or ham, poached egg, and hollandaise sauce
Toasted sourdough, smoked salmon, poached egg, and crème fraîche dressing
Toasted brioche, crispy streaky bacon, fried egg, and béarnaise sauce
Toasted muffin, prosciutto, soft-boiled egg, and beurre blanc sauce
correct: Toasted English muffin, Canadian bacon or ham, poached egg, and hollandaise sauce

question: What are the key ingredients in a classic French onion soup?
answers: Caramelised onions, beef stock, dry white wine or sherry, Gruyère, and croutons
Sliced white onions, chicken stock, cream, thyme, and melted Cheddar topping
Caramelised shallots, vegetable stock, brandy, Emmental, and sourdough toast
Caramelised onions, lamb stock, red wine, Comté, and toasted baguette slices
correct: Caramelised onions, beef stock, dry white wine or sherry, Gruyère, and croutons

question: What are the core ingredients in a classic beef bourguignon?
answers: Beef, red Burgundy wine, lardons, pearl onions, mushrooms, and bouquet garni
Beef, dark ale, carrots, parsnips, Worcestershire sauce, and fresh thyme
Beef, white wine, cream, tarragon, shallots, and button mushrooms
Beef, red wine, tomatoes, olives, anchovy paste, and herbes de Provence
correct: Beef, red Burgundy wine, lardons, pearl onions, mushrooms, and bouquet garni

question: What ingredients make up a classic carbonara?
answers: Pasta, guanciale or pancetta, eggs, Pecorino Romano, and black pepper — no cream
Pasta, streaky bacon, double cream, Parmesan, eggs, and black pepper
Pasta, smoked ham, mascarpone, Parmesan, white wine, and fresh parsley
Pasta, pancetta, cream, egg yolks, Gruyère, nutmeg, and white pepper
correct: Pasta, guanciale or pancetta, eggs, Pecorino Romano, and black pepper — no cream

question: What are the ingredients in a classic Bolognese (ragù alla Bolognese)?
answers: Beef mince, pork mince, soffritto, milk, white wine, tomato paste, and a little stock
Beef mince, chopped tomatoes, garlic, basil, red wine, and olive oil
Mixed mince, passata, mushrooms, red wine, herbs, and double cream
Lamb mince, fresh tomatoes, rosemary, red wine, olives, and anchovies
correct: Beef mince, pork mince, soffritto, milk, white wine, tomato paste, and a little stock

question: What are the core ingredients in a classic beef lasagne?
answers: Bolognese ragù, béchamel sauce, lasagne sheets, Parmesan, and mozzarella
Minced beef, tomato sauce, cream sauce, pasta sheets, and Cheddar
Beef ragù, velouté, egg pasta sheets, Gruyère, and breadcrumbs
Minced meat, passata, ricotta, lasagne sheets, and Pecorino Romano
correct: Bolognese ragù, béchamel sauce, lasagne sheets, Parmesan, and mozzarella

question: What is the primary raising agent in a Victoria sponge?
answers: Baking powder and the air beaten into the creamed butter and sugar
Bicarbonate of soda activated by acidic buttermilk in the batter
Beaten egg whites folded into the batter to provide lift
Self-raising flour only, with no additional leavening agent needed
correct: Baking powder and the air beaten into the creamed butter and sugar

question: What is the correct ratio of ingredients in a classic Victoria sponge?
answers: Equal weights of butter, caster sugar, eggs, and self-raising flour
Two parts flour to one part each of butter, sugar, and eggs by weight
Three parts flour to one part butter, with milk to adjust consistency
Equal weights of butter and sugar, with flour at double the weight of eggs
correct: Equal weights of butter, caster sugar, eggs, and self-raising flour

question: What is the difference between baking powder and bicarbonate of soda?
answers: Bicarbonate of soda needs an acid to activate it; baking powder contains its own acid and activates with moisture and heat
Baking powder is used for cakes only; bicarbonate of soda is used only in bread and savoury baking
Bicarbonate of soda produces more lift than baking powder and should be used in smaller quantities
They are chemically identical; the difference is only in the brand name and regional terminology used
correct: Bicarbonate of soda needs an acid to activate it; baking powder contains its own acid and activates with moisture and heat

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic shortcrust pastry?
answers: Plain flour, cold fat (butter or lard), a pinch of salt, and cold water to bind
Self-raising flour, room-temperature butter, egg yolk, and a little milk
Plain flour, warm butter, egg, baking powder, salt, and cold water
Strong bread flour, cold butter, a pinch of sugar, and full-fat milk
correct: Plain flour, cold fat (butter or lard), a pinch of salt, and cold water to bind

question: What is the fat-to-flour ratio in classic shortcrust pastry?
answers: Half fat to flour by weight (1 part fat : 2 parts flour)
Equal fat to flour by weight (1 part fat : 1 part flour)
Quarter fat to flour by weight (1 part fat : 4 parts flour)
Three-quarters fat to flour by weight (3 parts fat : 4 parts flour)
correct: Half fat to flour by weight (1 part fat : 2 parts flour)

question: What is choux pastry made from?
answers: Water, butter, plain flour, and eggs — the dough is cooked on the hob before baking
Milk, butter, self-raising flour, and eggs — mixed cold and piped directly onto trays
Water, lard, strong flour, and eggs — enriched with cream and chilled before shaping
Butter, plain flour, eggs, and baking powder — beaten until smooth then baked at high heat
correct: Water, butter, plain flour, and eggs — the dough is cooked on the hob before baking

question: What is the key technique that makes puff pastry flaky?
answers: Laminating — repeatedly folding butter into the dough to create hundreds of thin layers
Aerating — vigorously beating air into the fat before incorporating the flour
Chilling — resting the dough in ice water between each roll to firm the gluten
Hydrating — using a high proportion of water to steam the layers apart during baking
correct: Laminating — repeatedly folding butter into the dough to create hundreds of thin layers

question: What are the ingredients in a classic crème brûlée?
answers: Double cream, egg yolks, caster sugar, vanilla, and a caramelised sugar crust
Whole milk, whole eggs, icing sugar, vanilla, and a caramelised brown sugar top
Single cream, egg yolks, condensed milk, vanilla, and demerara sugar crust
Double cream, whole eggs, caster sugar, lemon zest, and a caramelised top
correct: Double cream, egg yolks, caster sugar, vanilla, and a caramelised sugar crust

question: What is the difference between a soufflé and a mousse?
answers: A soufflé is baked and relies on beaten egg whites for lift; a mousse is set cold and uses whipped cream or gelatin
A soufflé is always sweet; a mousse is always savoury and never contains eggs or cream
A soufflé is chilled until set; a mousse is baked and relies on steam for its light texture
Both are identical in technique but a soufflé uses a collar to rise above the ramekin
correct: A soufflé is baked and relies on beaten egg whites for lift; a mousse is set cold and uses whipped cream or gelatin

question: What does 'al dente' mean when cooking pasta?
answers: Cooked until tender but with a slight firmness remaining when bitten through
Cooked until completely soft all the way through with no resistance at all
Cooked until only just softened but still raw and starchy at the very centre
Cooked in heavily salted water until the pasta floats freely to the surface
correct: Cooked until tender but with a slight firmness remaining when bitten through

question: What is the purpose of resting meat after cooking?
answers: To allow juices to redistribute throughout the meat, resulting in a juicier and more evenly cooked result
To continue cooking the meat using residual heat to safely raise the core temperature
To allow the outer crust to firm up and the internal fat to resolidify before carving
To cool the meat surface quickly so the Maillard crust does not over-darken
correct: To allow juices to redistribute throughout the meat, resulting in a juicier and more evenly cooked result

question: What is the difference between stock and broth?
answers: Stock is made from bones and is rich in gelatin; broth is made from meat and has more flavour but less body
Stock is always seasoned; broth is always unseasoned and used as a base for sauces
Stock uses fresh vegetables; broth uses roasted vegetables and is therefore darker in colour
They are identical — the terms are interchangeable with no meaningful culinary distinction
correct: Stock is made from bones and is rich in gelatin; broth is made from meat and has more flavour but less body

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic pesto alla Genovese?
answers: Fresh basil, pine nuts, Parmesan, Pecorino, garlic, and extra virgin olive oil
Fresh parsley, walnuts, Gruyère, garlic, lemon juice, and vegetable oil
Fresh basil, almonds, Parmesan, garlic, lemon zest, and mild olive oil
Fresh rocket, pine nuts, Parmesan, garlic, capers, and extra virgin olive oil
correct: Fresh basil, pine nuts, Parmesan, Pecorino, garlic, and extra virgin olive oil

question: What are the ingredients in a classic guacamole?
answers: Ripe avocado, lime juice, salt, coriander, onion, and jalapeño or chilli
Ripe avocado, lemon juice, garlic, cumin, tomato, and Greek yoghurt
Mashed avocado, lime juice, sour cream, garlic powder, and pickled jalapeños
Ripe avocado, white wine vinegar, spring onion, parsley, and black pepper
correct: Ripe avocado, lime juice, salt, coriander, onion, and jalapeño or chilli

question: What are the key ingredients in a traditional Margherita pizza?
answers: Pizza dough, San Marzano tomato sauce, fresh mozzarella, and fresh basil
Pizza base, passata, shredded mozzarella, dried oregano, and olive oil
Flatbread, tomato purée, buffalo mozzarella, rocket, and Parmesan shavings
Pizza dough, fresh tomatoes, bocconcini, garlic oil, and dried basil
correct: Pizza dough, San Marzano tomato sauce, fresh mozzarella, and fresh basil

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic risotto?
answers: Arborio or Carnaroli rice, onion, white wine, hot stock, Parmesan, and butter (mantecatura)
Long-grain rice, shallots, dry white wine, chicken stock, cream, and Gruyère
Short-grain rice, leeks, vermouth, vegetable stock, mascarpone, and Pecorino
Arborio rice, garlic, rosé wine, hot fish stock, cream, and aged Cheddar
correct: Arborio or Carnaroli rice, onion, white wine, hot stock, Parmesan, and butter (mantecatura)

question: What is tempering chocolate?
answers: Melting and cooling chocolate to precise temperatures to stabilise the cocoa butter crystals, giving a glossy snap
Mixing chocolate with cream and butter at equal ratios to produce a smooth ganache for coating
Heating chocolate slowly in a double boiler until fully melted, then pouring into moulds to set
Adding a small amount of water to melted chocolate to prevent it seizing in a warm environment
correct: Melting and cooling chocolate to precise temperatures to stabilise the cocoa butter crystals, giving a glossy snap

question: What is ganache made from?
answers: Hot double cream poured over chopped chocolate and stirred until smooth
Melted chocolate whipped with cold butter until light and aerated
Chocolate melted in a double boiler with milk and a little glucose syrup
Equal parts cocoa powder and hot cream whisked together until thickened
correct: Hot double cream poured over chopped chocolate and stirred until smooth

question: What is the difference between single, double, and whipping cream?
answers: Single has ~18% fat and will not whip; whipping has ~36% fat; double has ~48% fat and whips thickly
Single has 10% fat, whipping has 25% fat, and double has 35% fat — all can be whipped with enough effort
Single cannot be heated; whipping is for cold uses only; double is suitable for both hot and cold uses
All three are interchangeable in savoury dishes; the distinction only matters in dessert preparations
correct: Single has ~18% fat and will not whip; whipping has ~36% fat; double has ~48% fat and whips thickly

question: What is caramelisation?
answers: The browning of sugars through heat, producing complex flavours and golden to dark brown colours
A reaction between proteins and sugars that creates the brown crust on seared meat
The process of dissolving sugar in water and cooking it to form a crystalline solid
The breakdown of starches into simple sugars through enzymatic activity at low temperatures
correct: The browning of sugars through heat, producing complex flavours and golden to dark brown colours

question: What is the difference between sea salt and table salt in cooking?
answers: Sea salt has larger, flakier crystals with a subtler flavour and is used for finishing; table salt is fine with additives and dissolves quickly for general cooking
Sea salt has more sodium per gram and is better for baking; table salt is coarser and better for seasoning at the table
They are chemically identical — the distinction is purely marketing with no practical effect on food
Table salt is always iodised which makes it unsuitable for fermenting; sea salt must be used for all preserved foods
correct: Sea salt has larger, flakier crystals with a subtler flavour and is used for finishing; table salt is fine with additives and dissolves quickly for general cooking

question: What are the key ingredients in a classic tartar sauce?
answers: Mayonnaise, gherkins (or capers), shallots, lemon juice, and parsley
Mayonnaise, horseradish, lemon juice, Dijon mustard, and dill
Mayonnaise, pickled jalapeños, lime juice, garlic, and fresh coriander
Crème fraîche, capers, anchovies, lemon zest, and fresh tarragon
correct: Mayonnaise, gherkins (or capers), shallots, lemon juice, and parsley

question: What is a gastrique in French cuisine?
answers: A sweet and sour reduction of caramelised sugar and vinegar used in savoury sauces, especially with game and duck
A cold sauce made by emulsifying butter into a reduction of white wine and cream
A concentrated reduction of fruit juice and red wine used to dress dessert plates
A slow-cooked mixture of onions, butter, and vinegar used as a condiment with charcuterie
correct: A sweet and sour reduction of caramelised sugar and vinegar used in savoury sauces, especially with game and duck

question: What is the difference between a stock and a glace (glace de viande)?
answers: A glace is a stock reduced by up to 90% until it becomes a thick, intensely flavoured syrupy gel
A glace is a lightly clarified stock used as a base; a stock is always reduced before use in sauces
A glace is made from fish and shellfish only; a stock can be made from any meat or vegetables
They are the same thing — the term glace simply indicates the stock has been skimmed and strained
correct: A glace is a stock reduced by up to 90% until it becomes a thick, intensely flavoured syrupy gel

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic potato dauphinoise?
answers: Thinly sliced potatoes, garlic, double cream, milk, salt, pepper, and nutmeg
Thinly sliced potatoes, chicken stock, butter, thyme, and gruyère cheese
Thinly sliced potatoes, crème fraîche, shallots, Dijon mustard, and Emmental
Sliced waxy potatoes, double cream, egg yolks, Parmesan, and bay leaf
correct: Thinly sliced potatoes, garlic, double cream, milk, salt, pepper, and nutmeg

question: What are the key ingredients in a traditional coq au vin?
answers: Chicken, red wine, lardons, pearl onions, mushrooms, garlic, and bouquet garni
Chicken, white wine, cream, shallots, tarragon, mushrooms, and butter
Chicken, dark beer, root vegetables, thyme, tomato paste, and bay leaf
Chicken, rosé wine, olives, artichokes, garlic, and herbes de Provence
correct: Chicken, red wine, lardons, pearl onions, mushrooms, garlic, and bouquet garni

question: What is the difference between chopping and mincing as knife techniques?
answers: Chopping produces rough irregular pieces; mincing produces very fine, almost paste-like pieces through repeated fine cuts
Chopping uses the tip of the blade; mincing uses the heel of the blade in a rocking motion
Chopping is always applied to meat; mincing is exclusively a vegetable preparation technique
Chopping produces larger pieces than dicing; mincing and dicing are interchangeable terms
correct: Chopping produces rough irregular pieces; mincing produces very fine, almost paste-like pieces through repeated fine cuts

question: What does 'folding' mean as a baking technique?
answers: Gently combining a lighter mixture (e.g. beaten egg whites) into a heavier base using a figure-of-eight motion to retain air
Vigorously stirring two mixtures together with a spoon or spatula until fully incorporated
Rolling a dough repeatedly over itself to develop gluten structure and even layering
Turning the edges of a pastry over a filling to seal the edges before baking
correct: Gently combining a lighter mixture (e.g. beaten egg whites) into a heavier base using a figure-of-eight motion to retain air

question: What are the ingredients in a classic brine for meat?
answers: Water, salt (typically 5–8% by weight), sugar, and aromatics such as peppercorns, bay, and herbs
Water, vinegar, salt, garlic, and spices cooked together and poured hot over the meat
Buttermilk, salt, garlic, and hot sauce used to tenderise poultry overnight before frying
Wine, olive oil, salt, lemon juice, and fresh herbs combined cold before soaking meat
correct: Water, salt (typically 5–8% by weight), sugar, and aromatics such as peppercorns, bay, and herbs

question: What is the difference between dry heat and moist heat cooking methods?
answers: Dry heat uses air or fat (roasting, grilling, frying, baking) and browns food; moist heat uses liquid or steam (poaching, braising, steaming) and does not brown food
Dry heat always requires a fat medium; moist heat always requires a water medium — neither method can be used without the correct medium
Dry heat is used exclusively for vegetables and grains; moist heat is used exclusively for proteins and pastry
Dry heat cooks food from the outside in; moist heat cooks food evenly from all sides simultaneously at the same rate
correct: Dry heat uses air or fat (roasting, grilling, frying, baking) and browns food; moist heat uses liquid or steam (poaching, braising, steaming) and does not brown food

question: What are the main spices in a classic garam masala blend?
answers: Cumin, coriander, cardamom, black pepper, cinnamon, cloves, and nutmeg
Turmeric, chilli, coriander, cumin, mustard seeds, and fenugreek
Paprika, cumin, oregano, garlic powder, cayenne, and cinnamon
Coriander, fennel, fenugreek, black pepper, mustard seeds, and curry leaf
correct: Cumin, coriander, cardamom, black pepper, cinnamon, cloves, and nutmeg

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic hummus?
answers: Cooked chickpeas, tahini (sesame paste), lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil
Cooked chickpeas, Greek yoghurt, cumin, garlic, lemon zest, and olive oil
Cooked white beans, tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and vegetable oil
Cooked chickpeas, cream cheese, roasted garlic, lemon juice, and parsley
correct: Cooked chickpeas, tahini (sesame paste), lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil

question: What does 'deglaze' mean when cooking?
answers: Adding liquid to a hot pan to dissolve the browned bits stuck to the bottom, creating a flavourful sauce base
Adding cold fat to a sauce off the heat to make it smooth and prevent it from splitting
Rapidly reducing a sauce over very high heat to concentrate the flavour and thicken the consistency
Removing the cooking liquid from a braise and skimming off the fat before reducing to a sauce
correct: Adding liquid to a hot pan to dissolve the browned bits stuck to the bottom, creating a flavourful sauce base

question: What is a gastronorm (GN) container used for in professional kitchens?
answers: A standardised stainless steel food pan used in steam ovens, bains-marie, and display fridges in defined fraction sizes
A temperature-controlled transport container for moving plated meals from the kitchen to service areas
A large non-stick cooking vessel used exclusively for making stocks and sauces in bulk quantities
A calibrated measuring jug used to ensure consistent portion sizes for liquids and sauces in service
correct: A standardised stainless steel food pan used in steam ovens, bains-marie, and display fridges in defined fraction sizes

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic lemon tart (tarte au citron)?
answers: Blind-baked shortcrust or sweet pastry shell, lemon juice, lemon zest, eggs, sugar, and butter
Sweet pastry, cream cheese, icing sugar, lemon curd, gelatin, and whipped cream
Pâte sucrée, lemon juice, condensed milk, egg yolks, and a meringue topping
Shortcrust pastry, lemon zest, double cream, whole eggs, caster sugar, and cornflour
correct: Blind-baked shortcrust or sweet pastry shell, lemon juice, lemon zest, eggs, sugar, and butter

question: What is the difference between steaming and boiling as cooking methods?
answers: Steaming cooks food above the water without contact, preserving more nutrients; boiling submerges food in water at 100°C
Steaming is only suitable for vegetables; boiling is required for proteins to ensure safe core temperatures are reached
Steaming uses a lower temperature than boiling and is therefore a slower method for all types of food
Boiling preserves more vitamins; steaming leaches nutrients into the water and is best avoided for vegetables
correct: Steaming cooks food above the water without contact, preserving more nutrients; boiling submerges food in water at 100°C

question: What is the purpose of blind baking a pastry case?
answers: To pre-cook the pastry base before adding a filling so it does not go soggy during the final bake
To test the pastry for any cracks before filling to prevent the filling leaking during cooking
To caramelise the inner surface of the pastry case so the filling does not stick to it
To allow the pastry to shrink fully before filling so it fits the tin precisely after cooling
correct: To pre-cook the pastry base before adding a filling so it does not go soggy during the final bake

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic risotto Milanese?
answers: Carnaroli rice, saffron, dry white wine, warm chicken or veal stock, Parmesan, and butter
Arborio rice, white wine, mushrooms, Parmesan, butter, and a pinch of nutmeg
Short-grain rice, saffron, cream, dry vermouth, onion, and aged Gruyère
Carnaroli rice, turmeric, white wine, warm vegetable stock, mascarpone, and Pecorino
correct: Carnaroli rice, saffron, dry white wine, warm chicken or veal stock, Parmesan, and butter

question: What is the smoke point and why does it matter when cooking with oil?
answers: The temperature at which an oil begins to burn and break down, producing harmful compounds and bitter flavours
The temperature at which oil becomes viscous enough to coat food evenly during a shallow fry
The point at which oil reaches the correct temperature for deep frying as indicated by a thin wisp of steam
The maximum temperature at which an oil remains liquid without solidifying in a cool kitchen environment
correct: The temperature at which an oil begins to burn and break down, producing harmful compounds and bitter flavours

question: What is a court bouillon?
answers: A quick-cook aromatic poaching liquid of water, white wine or vinegar, vegetables, and herbs used for fish and shellfish
A quickly made light chicken stock used as a base for velouté and cream soups in classic cuisine
A short reduction of red wine and shallots used as the base for a classic red wine butter sauce
A concentrated aromatic broth made from fish bones, white wine, and cream used in seafood bisques
correct: A quick-cook aromatic poaching liquid of water, white wine or vinegar, vegetables, and herbs used for fish and shellfish

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic crème pâtissière (pastry cream)?
answers: Milk, egg yolks, caster sugar, cornflour or plain flour, and vanilla
Double cream, whole eggs, icing sugar, gelatin, and vanilla extract
Milk, whole eggs, caster sugar, arrowroot, butter, and lemon zest
Single cream, egg yolks, cornflour, caster sugar, and orange blossom water
correct: Milk, egg yolks, caster sugar, cornflour or plain flour, and vanilla

question: What is the difference between a compote and a coulis?
answers: A compote is cooked fruit pieces in syrup; a coulis is a smooth sieved fruit purée
A compote is always cold; a coulis is always served warm alongside a hot dessert
A compote uses only stone fruits; a coulis is exclusively made from berries and citrus
They are interchangeable terms for the same fruit-based sauce in modern pastry
correct: A compote is cooked fruit pieces in syrup; a coulis is a smooth sieved fruit purée

question: What are the ingredients in a classic chimichurri sauce?
answers: Fresh parsley, garlic, oregano, red wine vinegar, olive oil, chilli flakes, and salt
Fresh coriander, lime juice, garlic, jalapeño, olive oil, and toasted cumin
Fresh herbs, anchovy paste, capers, red wine vinegar, Dijon mustard, and oil
Flat-leaf parsley, red pepper, onion, sherry vinegar, olive oil, and smoked paprika
correct: Fresh parsley, garlic, oregano, red wine vinegar, olive oil, chilli flakes, and salt

question: What is the purpose of adding salt to pasta water?
answers: To season the pasta from within as it cooks, since pasta absorbs water and flavour during cooking
To raise the boiling point of the water significantly, reducing the cooking time needed
To prevent the pasta from sticking together by changing the surface tension of the water
To activate the gluten in the pasta dough so it holds its shape throughout cooking
correct: To season the pasta from within as it cooks, since pasta absorbs water and flavour during cooking

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic French vinaigrette (sauce vinaigrette)?
answers: Dijon mustard, red wine vinegar, olive oil, salt, pepper, and optionally a little honey or shallot
Whole grain mustard, balsamic vinegar, walnut oil, garlic, and fresh tarragon
English mustard, white wine vinegar, sunflower oil, caster sugar, and chives
Dijon mustard, apple cider vinegar, extra virgin olive oil, garlic, and dried herbs
correct: Dijon mustard, red wine vinegar, olive oil, salt, pepper, and optionally a little honey or shallot

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic bread sauce?
answers: Whole milk, white breadcrumbs, onion studded with cloves and bay, butter, and seasoning
Double cream, fresh white breadcrumbs, garlic, thyme, and grated nutmeg
Whole milk, brioche crumbs, shallots, white pepper, cream, and a bay leaf
Single cream, stale white bread, onion, mustard powder, and ground mace
correct: Whole milk, white breadcrumbs, onion studded with cloves and bay, butter, and seasoning

question: What is the correct temperature for deep frying most foods?
What are the classic flavouring ingredients in a beurre blanc sauce?
answers: White wine, white wine vinegar, shallots, cold unsalted butter, and seasoning
White wine, lemon juice, shallots, clarified butter, cream, and fresh dill
Dry vermouth, shallots, cold salted butter, cream, and fresh chervil
White wine, shallots, fish stock, whole butter, and a pinch of cayenne
correct: White wine, white wine vinegar, shallots, cold unsalted butter, and seasoning

question: What is the ideal internal temperature for a medium-rare lamb rack?
answers: 57–60°C (135–140°F) after a brief rest
71–74°C (160–165°F) after a brief rest
49–52°C (120–125°F) after a brief rest
63–66°C (145–150°F) after a brief rest
correct: 57–60°C (135–140°F) after a brief rest

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic tapenade?
answers: Black olives, capers, anchovies, garlic, olive oil, and lemon juice
Green olives, sun-dried tomatoes, garlic, basil, and extra virgin olive oil
Black olives, roasted red peppers, capers, olive oil, and smoked paprika
Kalamata olives, walnuts, garlic, fresh thyme, red wine vinegar, and oil
correct: Black olives, capers, anchovies, garlic, olive oil, and lemon juice

question: What is the purpose of a water bath (bain-marie) in baking?
answers: To provide gentle, even heat that prevents egg-based mixtures from curdling or overcooking
To add steam to the oven to create a crisp crust on bread and enriched doughs
To maintain fat at a liquid state for continuous basting of roasting meats
To keep a sauce warm at a consistent temperature during a long service period
correct: To provide gentle, even heat that prevents egg-based mixtures from curdling or overcooking

question: What are the main ingredients in a classic sauce vierge?
answers: Ripe tomatoes, lemon juice, olive oil, coriander seeds, basil, and shallots
Cherry tomatoes, red wine vinegar, garlic, capers, tarragon, and olive oil
Concassé tomatoes, balsamic vinegar, basil, pine nuts, and extra virgin olive oil
Fresh tomatoes, lemon zest, olive oil, anchovy, oregano, and spring onion
correct: Ripe tomatoes, lemon juice, olive oil, coriander seeds, basil, and shallots

question: What does 'reducing' a sauce mean?
answers: Simmering or boiling it to evaporate water, concentrating flavour and thickening the consistency
Adding a starch-based thickener (cornflour or roux) to increase the sauce's viscosity
Straining it through a fine sieve to remove solid particles and produce a smooth texture
Cooling it rapidly over ice to stop the cooking process and prevent over-reduction
correct: Simmering or boiling it to evaporate water, concentrating flavour and thickening the consistency
